meta.title: Ernest — IGCSE/IB Entrance Readiness Diagnostic (Russian-L1 focus)
meta.revision: v1.1 (2026-07-01) — rebalanced MCQ answer positions to 3/3/3 across a/b/c and refreshed the first four stems after a position-A authoring artifact was caught immediately pre-run (correct option sat at 'a' in 8/9 MCQs; test-taker's first four 'a' answers were discarded as position-confounded).
meta.author: Claude Code (CLI session)
meta.template_lineage: Reuses the architecture of russian_l1_b2_diagnostic_v1 (clusters + per-item L1-trap IDs + recognition/production split + offline scoring), recalibrated for Ernest: B1 core with A2 floor probes and light B2 stretch; school/gaming/friends/sport/expat-school themes; ~22 items + one writing sample sized for a 30-minute sitting.
meta.purpose: Reveal Ernest's strengths and weaknesses across the highest-frequency Russian-L1 error zones that also matter for a Cambridge IGCSE / IB entrance test, ahead of his move from British School of Bahrain to Kazakhstan. Diagnostic, not drill — clean signal first, targeted practice after.
meta.context: Finished Year 10 at BSB; ~2 years English-medium schooling on top of Russian-medium background, so the foundation is thin in places. Level B1/B2 with a documented articles recognition-vs-production gap (≈100% MCQ vs ≈50% error-correction).
meta.level_design: B1 core, A2 floor probes (find where the foundation is thin), light B2 stretch (find the ceiling). Entrance-realistic pitch.
instructions_for_test_taker: One sitting, about 30 minutes. Work on your own — no dictionary, no help.
One answer per item. Don't go back and change earlier answers.
For the Russian sentences: write the most natural English version, not word-by-word.
If you truly have no idea, write PASS and move on. PASS is fine — it tells us what to practise.
The last task is a short piece of writing (6-8 sentences).
instructions_for_scorer: Score each item correct / partial / incorrect. Tag every miss with its l1_trap_id (or 'other').
Compute per-cluster correctness AND the recognition-vs-production split — the recognition-passes / production-fails gap is the key signal for Ernest (his known articles pattern).
Score the writing sample separately for L1-trap density (articles, tense, prepositions, word order) — not for style.
Feedback tier for the debrief: 'medium' (2-3 sentences + a brief Russian contrast), English coach language per Ernest's profile.
meta.answer_position_balance: Correct options are spread across positions a/b/c (3/3/3 over the 9 recognition MCQs) to prevent position-answering. Spread: a = e_art_01, e_wo_03, e_tense_01; b = e_tense_04, e_cond_01, e_ger_02; c = e_prep_01, e_count_02, e_art_04. Do NOT author with the correct option consistently first — the v1 Russian-L1 diagnostic hit exactly this artifact.

clusters.articles: No article system in Russian. Superlative 'the', abstract/uncountable zero-article, uncountable + no indefinite article, meal zero-article.
clusters.prepositions: Time in/on/at and fixed verb/adjective + preposition (good at, depend on) where Russian case government / prepositions don't transfer.
clusters.tense_aspect: Russian has no perfect or continuous aspect. Present perfect vs past simple, since/for, present continuous for actions now, basic irregular past.
clusters.word_order: Do-support in questions, indirect-question word order, adverb placement ('very like' calque).
clusters.countability_agreement: Uncountable-as-plural (informations), much/many, third-person do-support agreement (he don't).
clusters.gerund_infinitive: Closed-set gerund licensors (enjoy, look forward to) where Russian defaults to the infinitive.
clusters.conditionals: Russian single 'бы' form maps to English zero/first/second; 'will'/'would' leaking into the if-clause.

format_legend.gap_mcq: English stem with ___; choose one option.
format_legend.error_correction: Stem has one L1-typical error; rewrite it correctly.
format_legend.ru_to_en: Russian sentence; write the natural English.

items.stem: Football is ___ most popular sport at my school.
items.answer: the
items.explanation: Superlatives take 'the' — 'the most popular sport'. Russian has no articles, so the superlative word alone (самый популярный) feels complete and 'the' gets dropped.

items.answer: ate
items.explanation: Past of 'eat' is 'ate' (irregular). 'Eated' over-regularises; 'have eaten' can't go with 'yesterday' (a finished time). Floor check on basic past simple.

items.stem: We always go on holiday ___ summer.
items.answer: in
items.explanation: Seasons and months take 'in' — 'in summer', 'in July'. Compare 'on Saturday' (day), 'at seven' (clock time). Russian uses one preposition (в) for many of these, so in/on/at get mixed.

items.stem: If I ___ my homework now, my mum will be happy.
items.answer: finish
items.explanation: First conditional: 'if' + present simple, main clause 'will' — 'If I finish..., my mum will be happy'. Don't put 'will' in the if-clause. Russian marks the future in both halves.

items.stem: How ___ money do you spend on games?
items.answer: much
items.explanation: 'Money' is uncountable → 'how much money'. 'Many' is for countable plurals ('how many games'). Russian doesn't split much/many, so they get swapped.

options: I like this game very much.
items.answer: I like this game very much.
items.explanation: 'Very' can't sit in front of a verb — use 'very much' after the object (or 'really like'). 'I very like' calques Russian 'я очень люблю'.

items.stem: I usually have ___ breakfast at seven o'clock.
items.explanation: Meals take zero article — 'have breakfast / lunch / dinner'. This one usually transfers correctly from Russian, so it's a floor check.

items.stem: I'm looking forward to ___ my new classmates.
items.answer: meeting
items.explanation: In 'look forward to', 'to' is a preposition, so the next verb is -ing: 'looking forward to meeting'. It is NOT an infinitive 'to'. A common trap even at B2.

items.stem: I ___ this game before, so I know what to do.
items.answer: have played
items.explanation: Experience with no stated time → present perfect: 'I have played this before'. Russian has no perfect aspect, so it often collapses to past simple ('I played') even with no past-time marker.

items.stem: My little brother is very good in Minecraft.
items.answer: My little brother is very good at Minecraft.
items.explanation: 'Good at' something, not 'good in'. Russian 'хорош в' calques to 'good in'. English fixes the preposition to the adjective — 'good at', 'interested in'.

items.stem: Where you go to school now?
items.answer: Where do you go to school now?
items.explanation: English questions need do-support: 'Where do you go...'. Russian makes questions by intonation with no auxiliary, so 'Where you go...' is the L1 pattern.

items.stem: The teacher gave us a lot of informations about the exam.
items.answer: The teacher gave us a lot of information about the exam.
items.explanation: 'Information' is uncountable — no plural -s: 'a lot of information'. Russian 'информация' feels countable, producing 'informations'. Same family: advice, news, furniture, money.

items.stem: If I would have more time, I would learn to code.
items.answer: If I had more time, I would learn to code.
items.explanation: Second conditional: 'if' + past simple, main clause 'would' — 'If I had..., I would...'. 'Would' does not go in the if-clause. Russian puts 'бы' in both clauses, giving 'If I would have...'.

items.stem: I want to make games, so next year I will study the computer science.
items.answer: I want to make games, so next year I will study computer science.
items.explanation: School subjects and abstract fields take no article — 'study computer science', 'I like history'. The Russian-L1 trap is over-adding 'the' to a general uncountable idea.

items.stem: I know him since three years.
items.answer: I have known him for three years.
items.explanation: A state lasting up to now → present perfect + 'for' (a length of time): 'have known him for three years'. Russian uses the present tense here, giving the double error 'I know him since three years'.

items.stem: I enjoy to play basketball after school.
items.answer: I enjoy playing basketball after school.
items.explanation: 'Enjoy' takes the -ing form: 'enjoy playing'. Russian uses the infinitive after most verbs (люблю играть), giving 'enjoy to play'. Same group: finish, avoid, mind, keep + -ing.

items.answer: It depends on the teacher.
items.explanation: 'Depend on', not 'depend from'. Russian 'зависеть от' (literally 'from') calques to 'depend from'. The preposition is fixed by the English verb, not translated.

items.stem: I don't know where is the bus stop.
items.answer: I don't know where the bus stop is.
items.explanation: Embedded (indirect) questions keep normal statement order: '...where the bus stop is', not '...where is the bus stop'. This is one of the most common Russian-L1 calques.

items.stem: My best friend don't like football.
items.answer: My best friend doesn't like football.
items.explanation: Third-person singular needs 'doesn't': 'My friend doesn't like...'. Russian verbs carry no do-support, so 'don't' leaks in for he/she/it. Floor check on agreement.

items.answer: The teacher gave me good advice.
accept: The teacher gave me some good advice.
items.explanation: 'Advice' is uncountable — no 'a', no plural -s: 'good advice', not 'a good advice' / 'advices'. Russian 'совет' is countable, so the L1 pattern produces 'an advice'. 'The teacher' is a specific, known person = definite.

items.answer: Be quiet! The baby is sleeping.
accept: Be quiet, the baby is sleeping.
items.explanation: An action happening right now → present continuous ('is sleeping'), not present simple ('sleeps'). Russian uses one present tense for both, so 'sleeps' is the L1 default.

items.stem_ru: Если бы я был богатым, я бы купил новый компьютер.
items.answer: If I were rich, I would buy a new computer.
accept: If I was rich, I would buy a new computer.
items.explanation: Unreal present → second conditional: 'If I were rich, I would buy...'. Russian's single 'бы' maps here; 'were' is the textbook subjunctive and 'would' belongs only in the main clause.

items.stem: Write 6-8 sentences about your move to Kazakhstan. Tell us: what are you looking forward to at your new school? What do you think will be different from Bahrain? And what do you like doing in your free time?
items.scorer_note: No single answer — this is the free-production surface. Score for L1-trap density, not style. Watch specifically for: articles (a/the/zero on 'school', 'a new country', abstract nouns), tense choice (present habits vs future 'will/going to' vs any past), prepositions ('in Kazakhstan', 'at school', 'on the weekend', 'looking forward to'), word order, and gerund vs infinitive after 'like' / 'looking forward to'. Tag surfaced traps against the catalogue above and cross-check them with the item-level results.
